text: Which structure lines the inner surface of a synovial joint capsule and produces synovial fluid?
short_explanation: The synovial membrane secretes synovial fluid into the joint cavity.
long_explanation: The synovial membrane is a thin layer of connective tissue that produces lubricating fluid, reducing friction between the articular surfaces and nourishing cartilage.
clinical_application: Massage enhances circulation near joints, which may help maintain fluid exchange and reduce stiffness in clients with joint immobility.

text: Which type of cartilage covers the ends of bones in a synovial joint?
short_explanation: Articular cartilage is a type of hyaline cartilage that reduces friction in joints.
long_explanation: This smooth, resilient cartilage cushions joint surfaces and allows low-friction movement. It has no blood supply and relies on joint motion for nutrient exchange.
clinical_application: Massage supporting joint motion helps keep cartilage nourished indirectly via improved synovial fluid distribution.

text: What is the name of the fluid that lubricates and nourishes a synovial joint?
short_explanation: Synovial fluid allows smooth joint motion and carries nutrients to cartilage.
long_explanation: Synovial fluid is a thick, viscous substance secreted by the synovial membrane. It reduces friction, absorbs shock, and nourishes cartilage inside freely movable joints.
clinical_application: Joint mobilization and active movement during massage encourage synovial fluid movement for improved joint health.

text: Which part of the synovial joint provides mechanical strength and encloses the joint cavity?
short_explanation: The joint capsule is a fibrous structure surrounding the entire synovial joint.
long_explanation: The capsule includes an outer fibrous layer and an inner synovial membrane. It protects the joint, contains synovial fluid, and helps stabilize the articulation.
clinical_application: Massage improves tissue mobility around joint capsules, reducing stiffness and aiding recovery from immobility or postural restriction.

text: What is the function of a meniscus in a synovial joint?
abcd: C. Cushions and stabilizes joint surfaces
short_explanation: Menisci act as shock absorbers and stabilize articulating bones.
long_explanation: The meniscus is a fibrocartilaginous structure found in joints like the knee. It helps distribute load, absorb shock, and improve the congruency of articulating surfaces.
clinical_application: Meniscus injury can affect stability. Massage around supporting muscles may reduce strain on affected joints.

text: What is the function of a bursa in relation to synovial joints?
short_explanation: Bursae are fluid-filled sacs that reduce friction.
long_explanation: Bursae are small synovial sacs located between tendons and bones or muscles and skin. They prevent friction and inflammation during joint motion.
clinical_application: Massage near inflamed bursae should be cautious to avoid aggravation. Gentle techniques may reduce associated muscle tension.

text: Which connective tissue type primarily makes up ligaments in synovial joints?
short_explanation: Ligaments are composed of dense regular connective tissue.
long_explanation: This tissue is strong and organized in parallel bundles of collagen, ideal for resisting tension in one direction—key for joint stability.
clinical_application: Massage around ligaments may release muscle tension, but overstretching must be avoided in hypermobile clients.

text: Which structure helps prevent bone-on-bone grinding in a synovial joint?
short_explanation: Articular cartilage provides smooth surfaces at joint ends.
long_explanation: This cartilage layer reduces friction and distributes forces across joint surfaces. Its smooth, lubricated surface prevents painful grinding of bones.
clinical_application: Degeneration of articular cartilage, such as in osteoarthritis, can reduce joint mobility. Massage around affected areas may help manage pain.

text: What thickens into ligaments in some parts of the joint capsule?
short_explanation: The fibrous capsule may thicken to form intrinsic ligaments.
long_explanation: Portions of the fibrous joint capsule may differentiate into strong bands that reinforce joint stability, becoming intrinsic ligaments.
clinical_application: Massage therapists should be aware of capsular thickening and ligament stiffness in chronic joint conditions.

text: What is a primary function of synovial fluid?
short_explanation: Synovial fluid lubricates and nourishes articular cartilage.
long_explanation: This fluid allows free movement of joint surfaces while delivering nutrients to avascular structures like cartilage. It is essential for joint health and smooth motion.
clinical_application: Massage that encourages movement can help distribute synovial fluid more evenly and prevent stiffness.
